0---Problems/Circular-Averages.title: Circular Averages
0---Problems/Circular-Averages.content: For d \geq 2. let A_r denote the convolution operator with the surface measure of the circle of radius r at the origin. Then A_r is bounded on L^p(\mathbb{R}^d) for all 1 \leq p \leq \infty. We can also consider the Circular Maximal Operator operator A^* = \sup_{t > 0} A_r. Stein (d \geq 3) and Bourgain (d = 2) proved that A^* is bounded on L^p(\mathbb{R}^d) if and only if d/(d-1) < p \leq \infty.

0---Problems/Local-Smoothing-Inequality-.filePath: 0 - Problems/Local Smoothing Inequality For The Wave Equation.md
0---Problems/Local-Smoothing-Inequality-.title: Local Smoothing Inequality For The Wave Equation
0---Problems/Local-Smoothing-Inequality-.content:
In 1980, Akihiko Miyachi and Juan Carlos Peral separately proved that the half-wave propagator e^{it \sqrt{-\Delta}} on \mathbb{R}^d is bounded from L^p_s(\mathbb{R}^d) to L^\infty_{t,\text{loc}} L^p_x for s \geq (d-1)|1/p - 1/2|. In 1991, Andreas Seeger, Christopher D. Sogge, and Elias M. Stein extended this result to the study of more general half-wave propagators e^{it \sqrt{-\Delta_g}} with respect to Riemannian metrics g on a smooth manifold, and also obtain analogous results for a more general family of Fourier Integral Operators.
In 1991, Sogge noticed that the smoothness required of s can be reduced if one averages over a time interval, proving a quantitative improvement and using it to obtain a generalization of Jean Bourgain’s Circular Maximal Theorem. Sogge used some simple examples to conjecture a general range, namely, that the half-wave propagators are bounded from L^p_s(\mathbb{R}^d) to L^p_{t,\text{loc}} L^p_x if s > (d-1)|1/p - 1/2| - 1/p and p > 2n/(n-1). It is natural to conjecture that the same conjecture may also hold for half-wave propagators associated with more general Riemannian metrics. But in 1997 William P. Minicozzi II and Sogge showed that there exists Riemannian metrics where the conjecture fails for p \geq 2 + 8/(3d-3) when d is odd, and for p \geq 2 + 8/(3d-2) when d is even.
In 2000, Thomas Wolff introduced the first theory of decoupling inequalities for wave packets in order to prove the local smoothing conjecture in L^p where p is very large. This was sufficient to obtain results in geometric measure theory involving the Hausdorff dimension of sets which contain a 1-dimensional family of circles. In 2015, Bourgain and Ciprian Demeter proved the sharpest family of l^2 decoupling inequalities for the paraboloid and light cone, and as a consequence proved the local smoothing conjecture in Euclidean Space for p > 2 + \frac{4}{n-1}. In 2020, David Beltran, Jonathan Hickman, and Sogge adapted this approach to the variable coefficient setting, obtaining the required decoupling inequalities for Fourier integral operators and proving the local smoothing conjecture in the Riemannian setting in the same range as Bourgain and Demeter.
Using a refined decoupling estimate, Shengwen Gan, Danqing He, Xiaochun Li and Shukun Wu were able to obtain the local smoothing conjecture on Riemannian manifolds for p \geq 2 + 8/(3d-3) when d is odd (the sharpest possible estimates in the general setting), and when p \geq 2 + 8/(3n - 2 - \omega(d)), where \omega(d) = 4(7d-2)/(3d^2 + 5d + 14) when d is even.
In 2020, Larry Guth, Hong Wang, and Ruixiang Zhang completely resolved the local smoothing conjecture in the Euclidean plane by establishing a reverse square-function estimate for the wave propagators. This method was quickly adapted to the setting of two-dimensional Riemannian manifolds in a 2023 paper of Chuanwei Gao, Bochen Liu, Changxing Miao, and Yakun Xi.

0---Problems/The-Kakeya-Maximal-Inequali.title: The Kakeya Maximal Inequality
0---Problems/The-Kakeya-Maximal-Inequali.content:
The Kakeya maximal conjecture states that for a family \mathbb{T} of \delta-tubes in \mathbb{R}^n, which are direction separated, and for 0 \leq d \leq n,
\left\| \sum 1_T \right\|_{L^{\frac{d}{d-1}}(\mathbb{R}^n)} \lessapprox \delta^{1-n/d}.
Such a bound implies that all Kakeya sets must have Hausdorff dimension d.

0---Problems/The-Multilinear-Restriction.title: The Multilinear Restriction Conjecture
0---Problems/The-Multilinear-Restriction.content:
The restriction conjecture states that for 2d/(d-1) < q < \infty and  1 - \left( \frac{d+1}{d-1} \right) (1/q) \geq 1/p,
\| Ef \|_{L^q(\mathbb{R}^d)} \lesssim \| f \|_{L^p(\Sigma)}.
The multilinear restriction conjectures says that for 2 \leq k \leq n, if f_1,\dots,f_k are restricted to transverse subsets of \Sigma, then for q > 2(d+k)/(d+k-2),
\left\| \prod\nolimits_j |Ef_j|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^q(B_R)} \lessapprox \prod\nolimits_j \| f_j \|_{L^2(\Sigma)}^{1/k}.

The case k = 1 is essentially the Stein-Tomas theorem.
The conjecture has been proved for k = 2 by Tao.
The conjecture has been proved for k = n by Bennett, Carbery, and Tao.
The conjecture has been proved for k = n-1 by Bejenaru.

The methods of Bourgain and Guth show that multilinear estimates for the restriction conjecture imply some restriction estimates. In particular, if the multilinear restriction conjecture was established, then it would imply the results of Guth, who was able to get around using multilinear estimates by using the broad norms.

Apparently, the methods of Bennett, Carbery, and Tao imply the result is true for p > 2/(k-1).
Jennifer Duncan has claimed in talks to prove the result for p > 2(k+1)/k.

1---General-Zettels/0.-Questions.title: 0. Questions
1---General-Zettels/0.-Questions.content:
The linkage between fine scales and coarse scales is provided (heuristically, at least) by the
dispersion relation, which asserts that the frequency of a wave at fine scales determines the (group)
velocity of that wave at coarse scales; in the physical interpretation of the Schr¨odinger equation,
this relation is codified by de Broglie’s law p = ℏξ. To make this heuristic mathematically rigorous,
the wave packet decomposition is an ideal tool.
Did Fefferman 1970 use wave packets?
1991 - Bourgain reversed the Kakeya / Restriction correspondence. If you can understand Kakeya, you may be able to understand restriction better.

1---General-Zettels/Broad-Narrow-Analysi.title: Broad-Narrow Analysis
1---General-Zettels/Broad-Narrow-Analysi.content:
We aim to obtain a bound
\| Ef \|_{L^p(B_R)} \lessapprox \| f \|_{L^p(\Sigma)}
by using multilinear restriction estimates and decoupling estimates.
Fix K > 0, and cover B_R by balls B of radius A, where K \ll A \leq K^{O(1)}. Similarly, decompose \Sigma into a family \Theta of caps \theta of diameter 1/K. For each cap \theta, we let n(\theta) denote the normal vector to \Sigma at the center of the cap. We will pick K \lessapprox 1. In particular, it then follows that \#(\Theta) = K^{O(1)} \lessapprox 1, so that we can analyze each ball B separately and then put estimates together using the triangle inequality.
For each ball B, let S(B) be the set of all \theta \in \Theta so that
\| Ef_\theta \|_{L^p(B)} \gg \frac{\| Ef \|_{L^p(B)}}{\#(\Theta)}.
Then
\| Ef \|_{L^p(B)} \sim \left\| \sum\nolimits_{\theta \in S(B)} Ef_\theta \right\|_{L^p(B)}.
In fact, we have \| Ef \|_{L^p(B)} \approx \| Ef_\theta \|_{L^p(B)} for each \theta \in S(B).
Fix k \in \{ 2, \dots, d \}. We say a ball B is k-narrow if there exists a k-1 dimensional plane H in \mathbb{R}^d so that the set of vectors \{ n(\theta): \theta \in S(B) \} all make an angle at most 1/K with H. Otherwise, call the ball B a k-broad ball.
If B is a k-broad ball, then there exists k caps \theta_1, \dots, \theta_k in S(B) so that
|n(\theta_1) \wedge \cdots \wedge n(\theta_k)| \gtrsim 1/K.
We would like to obtain a bound of the form
\left\| Ef \right\|_{L^p(B)} \lessapprox \left\| \prod\nolimits_j |Ef_{\theta_j}|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(B)}
so that we can apply the multi-linear restriction theorem. The converse follows from Hölder’s inequality and that \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^p(B)} \sim \| Ef \|_{L^p(B)}, so we should think of this as a kind of reverse Hölder’s inequality.
If B were a ball of radius K, then by local constancy we could guarantee this reverse Hölder’s inequality, if we are willing to work with rapidly decaying cutoff functions around the balls B, rather than L^p norms on the balls themselves, i.e. proving a bound of the form
\left\| Ef \right\|_{L^p(w_B)} \lessapprox \left\| \prod\nolimits_j |Ef_{\theta_j}|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(w_B)}
Let us prove that
\left\| Ef \right\|_{L^p(w_B)} \lessapprox \left\| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^t |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{1-t} \right\|_{L^p(w_B)}
Using Bernstein’s inequality, we may assume without loss of generality that p \geq 2/t, since the result for any particular 1 < p < \infty implies the result for all p. Find a set S \subset \mathbb{R}^d so that \| 1_S \|_{L^1(w_B)} \geq 0.999 \| 1 \|_{L^1(w_B)} and for x \in S, |Ef_{\theta_2}(x)| \sim \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} K^{-d/p}. Then applying the general reverse Hölder’s inequality for L^p spaces, we have
\begin{align}
\left\| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^t |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{1-t} 1_S \right\|_{L^p(w_B)} &= \| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^{tp} |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{p(1-t)} 1_S \|_{L^1(w_B)}^{1/p}\\
&\geq \| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^{tp} \|_{L^{1/2}(w_B)}^{1/p} \| |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{p(1-t)} 1_S \|_{L^{-1}(w_B)}^{1-t}\\
&= \| Ef_{\theta_1} \|_{L^{tp/2}(w_B)}^t \| Ef_{\theta_2} 1_S \|_{L^{-p(1-t)}(w_B)}^{1-t}\\
&\sim \left[ K^{(d/p)(2/t - 1)} \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} \right]^t \left[ \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} K^{-d/p} K^{\frac{-d}{p(1-t)}} \right]^{1-t}\\
&\sim \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)}
\end{align}
We used the Sobolev embedding term to bound the first term in the second last line. But. now the general result follows by induction, since \prod_j Ef_{\theta_j} also satisfies the local constancy property.
For larger balls, this argument clearly cannot be generalized, but we instead use a random translation argument as a substitute. For each j, we can find a ball B_j of radius K so that \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^p(w_{B_j})} \geq (A/K)^d \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)}. If we consider vectors \nu_1,\dots,\nu_k chosen uniformly at random from B^*, then with probability \Omega((A/K)^{k-1}), the set S = \text{Trans}_{\nu_1} S_1 \cap \cdots \cap \text{Trans}_{\nu_k} S_k contains a ball B_0 of radius \Omega(K). Then by local constancy, \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^p(w_{B_0})} \gtrapprox (A/K)^d \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} for each j, and so by the reverse Hölder’s inequality we proved above, we conclude that
(A/K)^d \left\| Ef \right\|_{L^p(w_{B_0})} \lessapprox \left\| \prod\nolimits_j |\text{Trans}_{\nu_j} Ef_{\theta_j}|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(B_0)}
Thus we find that
\| Ef \|_{L^p(w_{B_0})} \lessapprox \mathbb{E}_\nu \left\| \prod\nolimits_j |\text{Trans}_{\nu_j} Ef_{\theta_j}|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(B_0)}.
Studying \text{Trans}_{\nu_j} Ef_{\theta_j} is no more difficult than studying Ef_{\theta_j} because we can write the translation into modulation of f_{\theta_j}, i.e. \text{Trans}_{\nu_j} Ef_{\theta_j} = E \{ e_{\nu_j} f_{\theta_j} \} where e_{\nu_j}(x) = e^{-2 \pi i \nu_j \cdot x}. We can then apply the multilinear inequality to each of the translations and thus obtain appropriate bounds.
The functions Ef_\theta are locally constant on balls of radius K, so the reverse would follow if A = K from local constancy. But for A \gg K the two quantities could be completely incomparable, since the functions |Ef_{\theta_j}| could be large where the other |Ef_{\theta_k}| are very small.
The fix for this is that, if we translate the functions Ef_{\theta_j} at random, then by local constancy there is probability O(K^{-O(1)}) that the regions where the functions are large all align. More precisely, by local constancy we can find a ball B_j \subset B of radius K upon which \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^p(B_j)} \gtrapprox \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^p(B)}. Then by local constancy (Bernstein’s inequality), \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^p(B_j)} \sim K^d \| Ef_{\theta_j} \|_{L^\infty(B_j)}
Then if \nu_1,\dots,\nu_k are vectors chosen uniformly at random from the ball of radius A, then with probability O(K^{-O(1)}), the set U = \bigcap_j \text{Trans}_{\nu_j} B_j satisfies U \gtrsim K.
Then for some \{ \nu_j \}, by local constancy,
\left\| \prod\nolimits_j \text{Trans}_{\nu_j} Ef_{\theta_j} \right\|_{L^p(U)}
If we define \text{Trans}_\nu Ef_{\theta_j}
local constancy only gives that
\| Ef \|_{L^p(B)} \sim \left( \sum \| Ef \|_{L^p(B_k)}^p \right)^{1/p} \sim \prod \| Ef_\theta \|_{L^p(B)}^{1/k} \lesssim A^d \prod \| Ef_\theta \|_{L^\infty(B)}^{1/k}
if l > 1 then the converse doesn’t quite work. The trick is to consider random translations
It then follows from a translation trick, Tacy’s theorem (we likely have to make another assumption on the caps so they don’t lie in a non-curved subspace), and a wave packet decomposition of each E f_{\theta_j} into tubes T_j that
\| Ef \|_{L^p(B)} \leq K^{O(1)} \left( \prod\nolimits_j \left\| \sum\nolimits_{\nu_j \in S(B,\theta_j)} f_{\theta_j,\nu_j} \right\|_{L^2(\Sigma)} \right)^{1/k} \leq K^{O(1)} \max\nolimits_j \left\| \sum\nolimits_{\nu_j \in S(B,\theta_j)} f_{\theta_j,\nu_j} \right\|_{L^2(\Sigma)}.
Since each \nu_j is in S(B,\theta_j) for at most K^{O(1)} different balls B, L^2 orthogonality of tubes implies that
\| Ef \|_{L^p(\text{Broad})} \leq K^{O(1)} \| f \|_{L^2(\Sigma)} \leq K^{O(1)} \| f \|_{L^p(\Sigma)}.
Now we deal with the narrow case. Suppose that B is a k-narrow ball. Then there exists
using decoupling inequalities. Suppose that B is a narrow ball, and S(B) is such that we have a decoupling inequality
\| Ef \|_{L^p(B)} \lesssim K^{O(1)} \left( \sum \| Ef_\theta \|_{L^p(B)}^2 \right)^{1/2}.
Summing over all narrow balls B using the finite overlap property, we obtain that
\| Ef \|_{L^p(\text{Narrow})} \lesssim K^{O(1)} \left( \sum \| Ef_\theta \|_{L^p(B_R)}^2 \right)^{1/2}.
We now bound each term \| Ef_\theta \|_{L^p(B_R)} using induction on scales. Namely, we can find a function g_\theta on \Sigma for each \theta so that \| g \|_{L^p(\Sigma)} = K^{\frac{d-1}{p}} \| f_\theta \|_{L^p(\Sigma)} and \| Ef_\tau \|_{L^p(B_R)} \lesssim K^{\frac{d+1}{p} - (d-1)} \| Eg \|_{L^p(B_{R/K})}. Applying induction on scales, letting C(R) be the best constant in the extension inequality on B_R, we find that
\| Ef \|_{L^p(\text{Narrow})} \lesssim K^{\frac{2d}{p} - (d-1)} C(R/K).
Putting together the analysis of the broad and narrow cases, we find that
C(R) \lesssim K^{O(1)} + C K^{\frac{2d}{p} - (d-1)} C(R/K).
Since p > 2d(d-1), for all \varepsilon > 0, if we choose K = R^\delta for \delta sufficiently small, depending on \varepsilon, we conclude that C(R) \lesssim_\varepsilon R^\varepsilon.
We likely can use decoupling in the right range provided that the k-1 dimensional hypersurface on which the caps are concentrated is appropriately curved. The amount of curvature required is not completely apparent I need to ask Jonathan what the range of decoupling estimates are known in the case that we have degenerate hyper-surfaces.
Guth Polynomial Partitioning Broad Narrow Argument
Fix K > 0, and divide a hypersurface \Sigma in \mathbb{R}^d into caps \tau of diameter O(K^{-1}). Given a function f on \Sigma, let g = Ef. Then a point x \in \mathbb{R}^d is \alpha broad if \max |Ef_\tau(x)| \leq \alpha |Ef(x)|. If a point is not \alpha broad, then it is \alpha narrow, which means there is some \tau_0 with |Ef(x)| \leq \alpha^{-1} |Ef_{\tau_0}(x)|. This gives a smaller scale which can bound the current scale of analysis, so induction on scales often allows us to bound the behavior of the extension operator at narrow points, and we may thus restrict our attention to the behavior at broad points.
More precisely, if we define \text{Br}_\alpha(g) to be equal to g at broad points, and equal to zero at narrow points, then we have a pointwise bound
 |g| \leq |\text{Br}_\alpha(g)| + \alpha^{-1} \max\nolimits_{\tau_0} |Ef_{\tau_0}|
Induction on scales bounds |Ef_{\tau_0}| uniformly, so it suffices to estimate |\text{Br}_\alpha(g)|. The advantage of reducing to the broad case is that one can often reduce to multilinear estimates, given that the caps \{ \tau_0 \} are suitably transverse to one another.
In more sophisticated contexts, it is useful to be more quantitative, especially when considering estimates closer to k-linear analysis. We define a measure \mu = \mu_a^p, locally constant at a scale K^2, so that for a  ball of radius K^2,
 \mu^p(f,B) = \min\nolimits_{V_1,\dots,V_a} \max\nolimits_{\tau \in \text{Tr}(V_1,\dots,V_a)} \int_B |Ef_\tau|^p
where V_1,\dots,V_a are k-dimensional subspaces, and \text{Tr}(V_1,\dots,V_A) is the set of caps transverse to all V_1,\dots,V_a, more precisely, making an angle greater than 1/K with each subspace. We then define, for a set U constant at a scale K^2,
 \| Ef \|_{\text{BL}_{k,a}^p(U)} = \left( \sum\nolimits_{B \subset U} \mu^p(f,B) \right)^{1/p}
The need to choose a > 1 is required so that the broad norm is semi-additive and satisfies the Holder inequality. We often choose a \sim 1. On a particular ball of radius K^2, we are only bounding those \| Ef_{\tau_0} \|_{L^p(B)} for which there exists \tau_1,\dots,\tau_{k-1} with \tau_0 \wedge \cdots \wedge \tau_{k-1} \gtrsim 1 and \| Ef_{\tau_i} \|_{L^p(B)} \gtrsim \| Ef_\tau \|_{L^p(B)}. The most useful property of the broad norm is that if we are only studying tubes that are tangent to a k-1 dimensional variety, then the broad norm of Ef is essentially zero (for whenever the tubes intersect, they do so in a way that is not k transverse).
In the multilinear case, what gets thrown out?

Guth uses the method to prove his L^\infty(\Sigma) \to L^p(\mathbb{R}^3) restriction estimate for p > 3.25.
Guth states that his broad narrow analysis is similar to the bilinear restriction methods of Tao, Vargas, and Vega, but I have not looked into this paper.

1---General-Zettels/Enemies-in-Multiline.content:
A regulus S is a degree 2 algebraic surface in \mathbb{R}^3 which is doubly ruled, i.e. there are two families of lines, which we might call horizontal and vertical, and each point lies in a horizontal line and a vertical line.

Planar Examples:
Reguli: Consider Wave Packets f = \sum f_T, where the sum is restricted to R^{1/2} \times R tubes taking from the ruling of S \cap B_R, with each wave packet having the same L^2 norm. This example is sharp for some bilinear restriction estimates.

1---General-Zettels/Induction-on-Scales.title: Induction on Scales
1---General-Zettels/Induction-on-Scales.content:
Suppose C(R) is a finite quantity chosen for each integer R > 0, such that, by some (hopefully trivial) argument, we can justify C(R) = R^{O(1)}. The goal of induction on scales is, by bounding C(R) in terms of C(R') for a small R', to obtain a bound C(R) \lesssim_\varepsilon R^\varepsilon for all \varepsilon > 0. We write A \lessapprox B if A \lesssim_\varepsilon R^\varepsilon B for all \varepsilon > 0.

If C(R) \lessapprox C(R^\alpha) for some 0 < \alpha < 1 and all \varepsilon > 0, and we know that C(R) \lesssim R^{O(1)}, then induction on scales can guarantee that C(R) \lessapprox 1. This is used, for instance in the Bennett Carbery Tao Multilinear Restriction Theorem.
If C(R) \lessapprox C(R^{1 - \delta}) + R^{O(\delta)} for all \delta > 0, then C(R) \lessapprox 1. This is used, for instance, in Tao Vargas Vega.
If C(R) \lesssim_\varepsilon (1 + \varepsilon) C(R/10) then we obtain that C(R) \lessapprox 1.

1---General-Zettels/Kolmogorov-Seliverst.title: Kolmogorov-Seliverstov-Plessner Linearization
1---General-Zettels/Kolmogorov-Seliverst.content: The act of studying a maximal operator \sup_\theta A_\theta on a space X by considering arbitrary functions \theta: X \to \Theta, and considering the linear operator A_\theta f(x) = A_{\theta(x)} f(x).

1---General-Zettels/Local-Constancy-Heur.filePath: 1 - General Zettels/Local Constancy Heuristics and the Uncertainty Principle.md
1---General-Zettels/Local-Constancy-Heur.title: Local Constancy Heuristics and the Uncertainty Principle
1---General-Zettels/Local-Constancy-Heur.content:
Let f be a function whose Fourier transform is supported on the ball \{ \xi : |\xi| \leq R \}. The local constancy heuristic, or the uncertainty principle, says that f is ‘locally constant’ at a scale 1/R. There are several ways of making this precise:
Bernstein’s Inequality
If a function f was locally constant at a scale 1/R, then on each ball B of radius 1/R, we might expect the function f as having a certain ‘height’ H. It would then follow that \| f \|_{L^p(B)} \sim H R^{-d/p}. In particular, this would imply that R^{d/p} \| f \|_{L^p(B)} \sim R^{d/q} \| f \|_{L^q(B)} for all 1 \leq p \leq q \leq \infty. This is, heuristically speaking, the case, if we replace local integrals on B with pseudo-local integrals. One direction of this inequality follows immediately from Hölder’s inequality. The other requires slightly more work.
First, Bernstein’s inequality says that
\| f \|_{L^{p,s}(\mathbb{R}^d)} \lesssim R^s \| f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^d)}.
But this equation can also be localized. Fix r > s, which can be arbitrarily large. For each B, let w_B = \left\langle  \frac{d(x,B)}{\text{Rad}(B)} \right\rangle^{-r}, and let \tilde{w}_B = \left\langle  \frac{d(x,B)}{\text{Rad}(B)} \right\rangle^{-t} for t \leq s-r. Then Bernstein’s inequality says that
\| f \|_{L^{p,s}(w_B)} \lesssim R^s \| f \|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)}.
To prove this result, we may assume by rescaling symmetries that R = 1. We then find a Schwartz function \chi with |\chi(x)| \geq 1 for |x| \leq 1, but with \widehat{\chi}(\xi) supported on |\xi| \lesssim 1. Applying the usual Bernstein inequality, if we let \chi_l(x) = \chi(x / 2^l), then we have
\| f \|_{L^{p,s}(w_B)} \lesssim \sum\nolimits_{l \geq 0} 2^{-lr} \| \chi_l f \|_{L^{p,s}(\mathbb{R}^d)} \lesssim \sum\nolimits_{l \geq 0} 2^{l(s-r)} \| \chi_l f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^d)} \lesssim \| f \|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)}
A similar argument using the Sobolev embedding theorem shows that the L^p improving inequality \| f \|_{L^q(w_B)} \lesssim R^{d(1/p - 1/q)} \| f \|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)} holds for p \leq q < \infty, where w_B and \tilde{w}_B are defined above, and where s = d(1/p - 1/q). In particular, since in this case s is always bounded by d. Customarily, one uses r > d so that the inequality holds also when q = \infty.
Pointwise Bounds
It follows from Bernstein’s inequality that if r > d, then for each ball B, and for 1 \leq p \leq q \leq \infty,
\| f \|_{L^q(B)} \lesssim R^{d(1/p - 1/q)} \| f \|_{L^p(w_B)}
Let us suppose that \| f \|_{L^p(w_B)} \lesssim \| f \|_{L^p(B)}. This is often the case in practical considerations whe analyzing balls B which are significant to the L^p norm of the entire function f, i.e. if \| f \|_{L^q(B)} \geq C^{-1} \| f \|_{L^q(\mathbb{R}^d)} for some fixed constant C. Then we have the inequality
\| f \|_{L^q(B)} \lesssim R^{d(1/p - 1/q)} \| f \|_{L^p(B)}.
In particular, f is bounded by H_B = R^{d/p} \| f \|_{L^p(B)} on B. But this implies that for any \varepsilon > 0, there exists \delta > 0, depending only on the implicit constants, such that all but a fraction \varepsilon of the points in B satisfy
\delta H_B \leq |f(x)| \lesssim H_B.
Thus we can think of f as having height H_B on almost all points of B, which is a kind of locally constant heuristic.
More generally, a version from this result follows if \| f \|_{L^p(w_B)} \sim \| f \|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)}, i.e. so that the L^p mass of f is not concentrated too far away from the ball B. We then conclude that for all \varepsilon > 0, there exists \delta > 0 so that for all but a fraction \varepsilon of the points in \mathbb{R}^d (with the fraction measured with respect to the weight w_B) satisfy \delta H_B \leq |f(x)| \lesssim H_B.

1---General-Zettels/Polynomial-Partition.title: Polynomial Partitioning
1---General-Zettels/Polynomial-Partition.content:
The idea of polynomial partitioning, introduced by Guth and Katz in their 2015 Paper on the Erdos distinct distances problem, is to combine the polynomial incidence geometric methods of Dvir with the divide and conquer approaches of divide and conquer partitioning approach introduced by CEGSW in incidence geometry.
Given a set X \subset \mathbb{R}^d of N points, the polynomial method allows us to find a polynomial of degree O(N^{1/d}) vanishing on X. In polynomial partitioning, for any D, we can choose a polynomial of degree D defining a hypersurface Z, such that \mathbb{R}^d - Z is divided into O(D^d) cells, each containing O(N/D^d) points from X. The surface Z is called the cell wall, and the connected components of \mathbb{R}^d - Z are called the cells. The problem then breaks down into two regimes:

If X contains few points in the cell wall, it must be evenly divided into each of the cells, so methods akin to  CEGSW apply.
If X contains many points in the cell wall, then we obtain structural information akin to Dvir’s Method.

The problem can also be applied to continuous problems. If f is a function on \mathbb{R}^d, then for any D we can find a hypersurface Z defined by a polynomial of degree D so that the L^1 norm of f on each cell cut out by the surface Z is the same. However, to fully exploit the polynomial structure in the continuous setting we must often thicken the cell wall, so that e.g. any ‘transverse’ tube passes through the surface Z at most D times, and thus enters at most D + 1 cells. If the tubes we are considering have thickness \delta, then we should let the \delta neighborhood Z_\delta of Z be the cell wall, and let the cells be the connected components of \mathbb{R}^d - Z_\delta. This is because it then follows that if a tube passes through a cell of \mathbb{R}^d - Z_\delta, then it’s central line passes through the corresponding cell in \mathbb{R}^d - Z, and can only do this at most D + 1 times.

1---General-Zettels/Reverse-Hölder's-Ine.filePath: 1 - General Zettels/Reverse Hölder's Inequality For Fourier Localized Functions.md
1---General-Zettels/Reverse-Hölder's-Ine.title: Reverse Hölder's Inequality For Fourier Localized Functions
1---General-Zettels/Reverse-Hölder's-Ine.content:
Suppose g_1,\dots,g_k are functions with Fourier transform supported on balls of radius R. Suppose also that w_B and \tilde{w}_B are weight functions, where w_B decays at a rate d times faster than \tilde{w}_B, then for any ball B of radius 1/R,
R^{d/p} \prod\nolimits_j \| g_j \|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)}^{1/k} \lesssim \left\| \prod\nolimits_j |g_j|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(w_B)}
In particular,
R^{d/p} \prod\nolimits_j \| g_j \|_{L^p(B)}^{1/k} \lesssim \left\| \prod\nolimits_j |g_j|^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(w_B)}.
This is called a reverse Hölder’s Inequality because if we flip the inequality and forget about weights, then the inequality is a special case of Hölder’s inequality.
Rescaling, we may assume R = 1. The result follows by applying the Pointwise Locally Constant Property, together with a union bound, to conclude that 99% of all points in \mathbb{R}^d, with respect to the weight \tilde{w}_B, satisfy |g_j(x)| \gtrsim R^{d/p} \| g_j \|_{L^p(w_B)}^{1/k} for each j, and for such points, we thus have \prod_j |g_j|^{1/k} \sim R^{d/p} \prod_j \| g_j \|_{L^p(w_B)}^{1/k}. Integrating over this region, the inequality immediately follows.
An Alternate Approach
This method may have technical problems. The result would follow if we could prove the bilinear result
\left\| f_1 \right\|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)} \| f_2 \|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)} \lessapprox \left\| |f_1|^t |f_2|^{1-t} \right\|_{L^p(\tilde{w}_B)},
since we could then apply induction. Using Bernstein’s inequality, we may assume without loss of generality that p \geq 2/t, since the result for any particular 1 < p < \infty implies the result for all p. Find a set S \subset \mathbb{R}^d so that \| 1_S \|_{L^1(w_B)} \geq 0.999 \| 1 \|_{L^1(w_B)} and for x \in S, |Ef_{\theta_2}(x)| \sim \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} K^{-d/p}. Then applying the general reverse Hölder’s inequality for L^p spaces, we have
\begin{split}
\left\| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^t |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{1-t} 1_S \right\|_{L^p(w_B)} &= \| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^{tp} |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{p(1-t)} 1_S \|_{L^1(w_B)}^{1/p}\\
&\geq \| |Ef_{\theta_1}|^{tp} \|_{L^{1/2}(w_B)}^{1/p} \| |Ef_{\theta_2}|^{p(1-t)} 1_S \|_{L^{-1}(w_B)}^{1-t}\\
&= \| Ef_{\theta_1} \|_{L^{tp/2}(w_B)}^t \| Ef_{\theta_2} 1_S \|_{L^{-p(1-t)}(w_B)}^{1-t}\\
&\sim \left[ K^{(d/p)(2/t - 1)} \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} \right]^t \left[ \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)} K^{-d/p} K^{\frac{-d}{p(1-t)}} \right]^{1-t}\\
&\sim \| Ef \|_{L^p(w_B)}
\end{split}
We used the Sobolev embedding term to bound the first term in the second last line. But. now the general result follows by induction, since \prod_j Ef_{\theta_j} also satisfies the local constancy property.

1---General-Zettels/Shadings.title: Shadings
1---General-Zettels/Shadings.content:
Suppose we have a family of subsets \mathbb{A} in \mathbb{R}^n, with |A| \sim V for each A \in \mathbb{A}. Suppose we are trying to prove an inequality
\left\| \sum\nolimits_{A \in \mathbb{A}} 1_A \right\|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)} \lesssim C. \tag{1}
By real interpolation, (1) is roughly equivalent to a restricted bound of the form
\sum |A \cap X| \leq C |X|^{1/d}, \tag{2}
where d = p'. By dyadic pigeonholing, we may find \mathbb{A}' \subset \mathbb{A} and \lambda \in (0,1) so that |A \cap X| \sim \lambda |A| for each A \in \mathbb{A}', and such that \sum_{A \in \mathbb{A}} |A \cap X| \approx \sum_{A \in \mathbb{A}'} |A \cap X|. Normally we have \# \mathbb{A} \lesssim V^{-1}, so that M = (\# (\mathbb{A}') V)^{-1} is large. Then, rearranging, we find (2) is equivalent to
|X| \gtrapprox \left( \lambda / CM \right)^d. \tag{3}
We can state this process in the language of shadings. For each A \in \mathbb{A}, we pick X(A) \subset A so that X(A) \sim \lambda |A| for each A. Such a choice is called a \lambda-shading. The L^p inequality we wished to prove above is equivalent to proving that for any subset \mathbb{A}' of \mathbb{A}, and any \lambda-shading of \mathbb{A}', the set X = \bigcup X(A) satisfies |X| \gtrapprox (\lambda / CM)^d. Thus we see that upper bounds on L^p sum of indicator functions are roughly equivalent to lower bounding the size of shadings, which is a Kakeya type problem.
The Generic Multiplicity of Shadings
Suppose that each set in \mathbb{A} is \delta-discretized (roughly speaking, a union of \delta balls). For each \delta ball Q, let M(Q) denote the number of sets in \mathbb{A} containing Q. Then Markov’s inequality, applied to (1), implies that
\# \{ Q : M(Q) \geq M_0 \} \leq \delta^{-n} C^p / M_0^p. \tag{4}
If X is a \delta-discretized \lambda-shading of \mathbb{A}', then (3) implies that X contains at least \delta^{-n} (\lambda / CM)^d balls of radius \lambda. So (3) and (4) together imply that at least half the cubes in X have multiplicity at most O( C^d (M/\lambda)^{d - 1} ). Conversely, if (3) and (4) hold, then we can obtain inequalities of the form (1) because Removing Exceptional Sets From Lp Estimates.
The Special Case of Tubes
In the special case where we are studying the Kakeya inequality, \mathbb{A} is a family of direction separated \delta-tubes, V = \delta^{1-n}, and C = \delta^{1 - n/d}, The Random Rotations Trick allows us to assume that M \sim 1, so the The Kakeya Maximal Inequality is roughly equivalent to prove that for any family \mathbb{T} of \sim \delta^{1-n} direction separated \delta tubes, and any \lambda-shading X of \mathbb{T}, |X| \gtrapprox \lambda^d \delta^{n-d}. In particular, at least half the cubes in any \lambda-shading of \mathbb{T} must have multiplicity at most O(\delta^{d - n} \lambda^{1-d}).
Geometric Means
Suppose we have proved
\left\| \left( \sum\nolimits_{A_1 \in \mathbb{A}, \dots, A_k \in \mathbb{A}_k} 1_{A_1} \cdots 1_{A_k} \right)^{1/k} \right\|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)} \lesssim C. \tag{1}
where \mathbb{A}_1,\dots, \mathbb{A}_k are families of \delta discretized sets. The analogous weak bound is that if we let M_i(Q) denote the number of elements of \mathbb{A}_i which contain a \delta ball Q, then for any \delta discretized set X,
\sum\nolimits_{Q \subset X} [M_1(Q) \cdots M_k(Q)]^{1/k} \lesssim C.
Pigeonholing, we may assume that X is a \lambda-shading of the set of intersections
\mathbb{A} = \{ A_1 \cap \cdots \cap A_k : A_1 \in \mathbb{A}_1, \dots, A_k \in \mathbb{A}_k \}.

1---General-Zettels/Simultaneous-Saturat.title: Simultaneous Saturation
1---General-Zettels/Simultaneous-Saturat.content:
Suppose that T_1,\dots,T_M are operators, with T_i mapping elements of a Hilbert space H_i to functions on a set X, where \| T_i \|_{H_i \to L^2(X)} \lesssim B. For each x, we consider the linear functional T_i(x) on H_i given by T_i(x) \{ v \} = (T_i v)(x). We identify T_i(x) with the corresponding vector in H_i which gives the linear functional via the inner product. Also fix a parameter \lambda > 0.
Let us assume (a) That \langle T_i(x), T_i(y) \rangle \leq B^2 for each i, and each x and y in X, (b) that there is a relationship C_i on X for each i such that if C_i(x,y) does not hold, then \langle T_i(x), T_i(y) \rangle \leq R(\lambda), and (c) for each m \leq M, the only elements of the set of weavings
\{ (x_1,\dots,x_m): C_1(x_1,x_2), C_2(x_2,x_3),\dots, C_m(x_m,x_1) \}
are the trivial solutions x_1 = \dots = x_m.
Theorem Let N be the largest integer for which there exists N distinct points x_1,\dots,x_N so that
\left| \prod\nolimits_{i = 1}^M (T_i v_i)(x_j) \right|^{1/M} \geq L \left( \prod\nolimits_{i = 1}^M \| v_i \|_{H_i} \right)^{1/M} \quad\text{for each $j \in \{ 1, \dots, N \}$}.
Suppose we a priori know some upper bound N \leq R(\lambda)^{-\varepsilon/M^2}, for some \varepsilon > 0. Then there exists C independent of B, L, and \lambda so that
N \leq \left( C (B/L)^{2 \left( \frac{M}{M-1} \right)} \right)^{\frac{1}{1 - \varepsilon}}.
Proof: Consider the linear map T from H_N = (\prod_i H_i)^N to functions on X^{MN} given by
Tv(x) = \prod\nolimits_{i = 1}^M \prod\nolimits_{j = 1}^N (T_i v_{ij})(x_{ij})
Let v \in (\prod_i H_i)^N be the vector with v_{ij} = v_i for each j \in \{ 1, \dots, N \}, and let x \in X^{MN} be the set of all points with x_{ij} = x_j for each i \in \{ 1, \dots, M \}. Let S_N be the permutation group on N elements. For any \sigma \in S_N^M, let x^\sigma_{ij} = x_{i \sigma(j)} = x_{\sigma(j)}. Then Tv(x^\sigma) = Tv(x) for each \sigma. Each permutation gives rise to a distinct point since the points x_1, \dots, x_N are distinct. Let S be the set of all x^\sigma. Then \#(S) = M \cdot N!. We will identify S with the set S_N^M.
We give S the uniform probability measure, so that we can consider the Hilbert space L^2(S). We note then that if 1_S \in L^2(S) is the constant function, then for any unit vector v \in H_N,
L^{MN} \leq |\langle Tv, 1_S \rangle| = |\langle v ,  T^* 1_S \rangle| \leq \| T^* 1_S \|_{H_N} = \langle TT^* 1_S, 1_S \rangle^{1/2}.
One may calculate that
(TT^* f)(x) = \sum U(x,y) f(y)
where
U(x,y) = \prod\nolimits_{i,j} \langle T_i(x_{ij}), T_i(y_{ij}) \rangle.
Lemma: 1_S is an eigenfunction of TT^*.
Proof: This follows because \sum U(x,y) = \sum U(x^\sigma, y) for each \sigma \in S_N^M, as the two sums are just permutations of one another.
If TT^* 1_S = \Lambda 1_S, then we conclude that L^{2MN} \leq \Lambda since we know, from the calculation above, that  \langle T1_S, T1_S \rangle \geq L^{2MN}.
We now produce a weaving, i.e. a path of points connected by the relations \{ C_i \}. If U(x^\sigma, x^\tau) avoids all decay, then for each i and j, the relation C_i(x_{\sigma_i(j)}, x_{\tau_i(j)}) cannot hold. For simplicity, consider the case where M is even. Setting i = 1, we conclude C_1(x_{\sigma_1(j)}, x_{\tau_1(j)}) holds for all j. Write \tau_1 = \tau_2 (\tau_2^{-1} \tau_1). Since the relation C_2 is symmetric, we have
C_2(x_{\tau_2(j')}, x_{\sigma_2(j')}) \quad\text{for all $j'$}
and in particular if we let j' = \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1(j) then we conclude C_2(x_{\tau_1(j)}, x_{\sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1(j)}) holds. Writing \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1 = \sigma_3 \sigma_3^{-1} \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1, we have
C_3(x_{\sigma_3(j')}, x_{\tau_3(j')}) \quad\text{for all $j'$}
and so setting j' = \sigma_3^{-1} \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1, we find that
C_3(x_{\sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1(j)}, x_{\tau_3 \sigma_3^{-1} \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1})
we repeat this process for i = 1 to i = M. If it is true that \sigma_1 \to_{C_1} \tau_1 \to_{C_2} \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1 \to_{C_3} \tau_3 \sigma_3^{-1} \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1. If M is even, then the last permutation will be
\sigma_M \tau_M^{-1} \tau_{M-1} \sigma_{M-1}^{-1} \sigma_{M-2} \cdots \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1
and if this permutation is equal to \sigma_1, then the loop closes, i.e. the set of all pairs \sigma and \tau such that we do not have decay must satisfy the equation F(\sigma,\tau) = \text{id}, where \text{id} \in S_N is the identity is the identity, and we consider our indices \{ 1, \dots, M-1 \} modulo M, then we can write
F(\sigma,\tau) = \sigma_1^{-1} \sigma_M \tau_M^{-1} \tau_{M-1} \sigma_{M-1}^{-1} \sigma_{M-2} \cdots \sigma_2 \tau_2^{-1} \tau_1 = \prod\nolimits_{i=1}^{M-1} \sigma_{i+2}^{-1} \sigma_{i+1} \tau_{i+1}^{-1} \tau_i
Consider the binary relation R defined on S_N such that R(\sigma,\tau) holds if \sigma^{-1} \sigma = \tau^{-1} \tau. Then define a relation \text{Even} on S_N^M, such that \text{Even}(\sigma,\tau) holds if for each even index i \leq M, R(\sigma_i,\tau_i), and define \text{Odd} such that \text{Odd}(\sigma,\tau) holds if for each odd index i. \leq M, R(\sigma_i,\tau_i). Let A_{\text{Even}} and A_{\text{Odd}} be the matrix representations of \text{Even} and \text{Odd}, and let C be the matrix representation of the relation \text{Both}(\sigma,\tau) = \text{Even}(\sigma,\tau) \wedge \text{Odd}(\sigma,\tau).
Lemma C A_{\text{Even}} C^{-1} = A_{\text{Odd}} and C^2 commutes with both A_{\text{Even}} and A_{\text{Odd}}.
Proof: This follows immediately from the fact that C(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_M) = (\sigma_2,\dots,\sigma_M,\sigma_1).
Lemma The matrix A = A_{\text{Even}} A_{\text{Odd}} + A_{\text{Odd}} A_{\text{Even}} is positive semidefinite.
Proof: The matrices A_{\text{Even}} and A_{\text{Odd}} are both projection matrices onto subspaces V_{\text{Even}} and V_{\text{Odd}}. Thus (A_{\text{Even}} + A_{\text{Odd}})^2 = A_{\text{Even}} + A_{\text{Odd}} + A. So it suffices to show that all the non-zero eigenvalues of A_{\text{Even}} + A_{\text{Odd}} are at least one.
Now A_{\text{Even}} + A_{\text{Odd}} = A_{\text{Even}} + C A_{\text{Even}} C^{-1}, and using this and that C^2 commutes we find that C commutes with A_{\text{Even}} and A_{\text{Odd}}. Let v be a unit eigenvector for C and A_{\text{Even}} + A_{\text{Odd}} with eigenvalue \kappa_A. Since C^M = I, Cv = e^{i \theta} v for some Mth root of unity e^{i \theta}. If w = \pi_{\text{Even}}(v) then
\begin{align*}
\kappa v &= (A_{\text{Even}} + A_{\text{Odd}})v\\
&= A_{\text{Even}} v + e^{-i \theta} C A_{\text{Even}} v\\
&= w + e^{-i \theta} C w
\end{align*}
Note that Cw \in V_{\text{Odd}}. Now
\begin{align*}
\kappa &= \langle v, w + e^{-i \theta} C w \rangle\\
&= \langle 
\end{align*}

1---General-Zettels/The-Polynomial-Wolff.title: The Polynomial Wolff Axioms
1---General-Zettels/The-Polynomial-Wolff.content:
A set of \delta-tubes \mathbb{T} satisfy the Polynomial Wolff axioms if, for any semi-algebraic set S,
\# \{ T \in \mathbb{T}: |T \cap S| \geq \lambda |T| \} \lesssim |S| \delta^{1-n} \lambda^{-n} \tag{1}
where the implicit constants depend only on the complexity of the semi-algebraic set S. Guth, Zahl, Katz, and Rogers proved that a family of tubes pointing in a \delta-separated family of directions satisfy the Polynomial Wolff Axioms.
Let us say a tube T is tangent to a \delta neighborhood S of an algebraic surface \Sigma if |T \cap S| \geq 0.1 |T|. Since |S|  \lesssim \delta the Polynomial Wolff Axioms tell us that at most O(\delta^{2-n}) tubes in \mathbb{T} are tangent to the surface. If \mathbb{T} are \delta separated, we can have \#(\mathbb{T}) \sim \delta^{1-n}, and so the Polynomial Wolff Axioms tell us most of the tubes in \mathbb{T} are not tangent to S.
Suppose \mathbb{T} contains at most m tubes in each direction. A natural question is to understand how (1) may be quantified in terms of m - this may have applications to restriction theory, e.g. to the methods of Wang and Wu.

1---General-Zettels/The-Refined-Decoupli.title: The Refined Decoupling Theorem
1---General-Zettels/The-Refined-Decoupli.content:
Suppose p > 2(d+1)/(d-1).
Let S be a strictly convex, C^2 hypersurface in \mathbb{R}^d with Gaussian curvature \sim 1, and consider f: S \to \mathbb{C} that can be expanded in a wave-packet decomposition of the form f = \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}} f_T, where \mathbb{T} is a family of R-tubes, so that f_T is supported in a radius R^{-1} neighborhood of S, and E_S f_T is essentially supported on T. Assume that the w_R norms of the functions \{ E_S f_T: T \in \mathbb{T} \} are all comparable (Equivalently, we may assume the norms of the \{ f_T : T \in \mathbb{T} \} are all comparable). Suppose X \subset B_R is a union of a family of radius R^{1/2} balls, so that each such ball Q in the union has multiplicity m(Q) = \# \{ T \in \mathbb{T}: Q \cap T \neq \emptyset \} at most M. Then
\| E_S f \|_{L^p(X)} \lessapprox M^{\frac{2}{n-1}} \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \| E_S f_T \|_{L^p(w_R)}^2.

1---General-Zettels/The-Wave-Packet-Deco.filePath: 1 - General Zettels/The Wave Packet Decomposition For Extension Operators.md
1---General-Zettels/The-Wave-Packet-Deco.title: The Wave Packet Decomposition For Extension Operators
1---General-Zettels/The-Wave-Packet-Deco.content:
Given any function f supported on a compact neighborhood of a curved hypersurface \Sigma, and each R > 0, we can break \Sigma down finitely overlapping caps \Theta, each having dimensions R^{-1/2} tangent to \Sigma, and dimension R^{-1} in the normal direction to \Sigma. If, for each \theta \in \Theta, we consider a family of tubes \mathbb{T}(\theta) pointing in the normal direction to the cap, with dimensions R^{1/2} by R, and then write \mathbb{T} = \bigcup \mathbb{T}(\theta), then we have an orthogonal decomposition f = \sum f_T, where for T \in \mathbb{T}(\theta) the following is true:

f_T is supported on \theta.
For x \in B_R, E_\Sigma f_T(x) \approx a_T \chi_T e^{2 \pi i \omega_\theta x}, where \omega_\theta is the center of \theta, |a_T| \sim R^{-1/2} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\Sigma)}, and \chi_T is smooth and adapted to T. In particular, \| E_\Sigma f_T \|_{L^2(B_R)} \sim R^{1/2} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\Sigma)}.
The functions E f_T are locally orthogonal on balls of radius R^{1/2}. That is, for any ball B of radius at least R^{1/2}, \| Ef \|_{L^2(B)} \approx \sum \| Ef_T \|_{L^2(B)}^2.

The method was widely brought to the attention by Bourgain’s use of the result in a 1991 paper (some claim he introduced the method, though it is also in use by Fefferman and Cordoba in their work on the restriction problem), and is highly similar to the second dyadic decomposition introduced in 1991 by Seeger, Sogge and Stein to analyze Fourier Integral Operators, i.e. it is precisely the scale at which the phase function in the extension operator linearizes, or equivalently, the largest rectangular boxes contained in a 1/R-neighborhood of the surface \Sigma.

1---General-Zettels/Wang-Wu-Restricted-D.filePath: 1 - General Zettels/Wang-Wu Restricted Decoupling + Kakeya Implies Restriction.md
1---General-Zettels/Wang-Wu-Restricted-D.title: Wang-Wu Restricted Decoupling + Kakeya Implies Restriction
1---General-Zettels/Wang-Wu-Restricted-D.content:
Recall the The Refined Decoupling Theorem. In this note we discuss how to use this theorem, together with Kakeya type bounds, to prove Restriction Theorems. To begin with, we note that the refined decoupling theorem implies the following bound.
Theorem: Suppose a family of tubes \mathbb{T} can be divided into families of tubes \mathbb{T}_\theta which are R^{-1/2} direction separated, and \#(\mathbb{T}_\theta) \leq m for each \theta. Then if X is a union of R^{1/2} balls, so that m(Q) \leq M for each Q \subset X, then
\| E_S f \|_{L^p(X)} \lessapprox R^{\frac{n}{p} - \frac{n-1}{2}} M^{\frac{1}{p} \frac{2}{n-1}} m^{1/p - 1/2} \| f \|_{L^p(M)}.
Proof: Applying the The Refined Decoupling Theorem, we find that
\| E_S f \|_{L^p(X)}^p \lessapprox M^{\frac{2}{n-1}} \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \| E_S f_T \|_{L^p(w_R)}^p.
Now \| E_S f_T \|_{L^p(w_R)}^p \sim R^{ \frac{p - p_{ST}}{2 - p_{ST}} } \| f_T \|_{L^2(M)}^p for each T \in \mathbb{T}, so
\sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \| E_S f_T \|_{L^p(w_R)}^p \sim R^{\frac{p - p_{ST}}{2 - p_{ST}}} \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \| f_T \|_{L^2(M)}^p.
Since all the norms \| f_T \|_{L^2(M)} are all comparable, we have that for each \theta,
\sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}_\theta} \| f_T \|_{L^2(M)}^p \sim m^{1 - p/2} \left( \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}_\theta} \| f_T \|_{L^2(M)}^2 \right)^{p/2} \sim m^{1 - p/2} \| f_\theta \|_{L^2(M)}^p.
By H”{o}lder’s inequality, since f_\theta is supported on a R^{-1/2} cap, we have
\| f_\theta \|_{L^2(M)}^p \lesssim R^{- \left( \frac{n-1}{2} \right) (p/2 - 1)} \| f_\theta \|_{L^p(M)}^p.
Combining these bounds gives that
\begin{split}
    \| E_S f \|_{L^p(X)}^p &\lessapprox M^{\frac{2}{n-1}} m^{1 - p/2} R^{\frac{p - p_{ST}}{2 - p_{ST}} - \left( \frac{n-1}{2} \right) (p/2 - 1) } \| f \|_{L^p(M)}^p\\
    &= R^{n - \left( \frac{n-1}{2} \right) p} M^{\frac{2}{n-1}} m^{1 - p/2} \| f \|_{L^p(M)}^p.
\end{split}
So, if we can show a potential counterexample to a restriction bound is concentrated on a set X upon which M^{\frac{2}{n-1}} m^{1 - p/2} \lessapprox R^{ \left( \frac{n-1}{2} \right) p - n }, then we will have obtained a contradiction, thus proving the restriction bound. □
In particular, if p = p_{ST} then the bound becomes
 \| E_S f \|_{L^{p_{ST}}(X)} \lesssim R^{-1/p_{ST}} (M/m)^{\frac{1}{n+1}} \| f \|_{L^p(M)},
which will be important in a later calculation.
For a given family of tubes \mathbb{T}, controlling M and m simultaneously is a Kakeya type problem. If we consider a set X formed from the union of R^{1/2} tubes, such that |X \cap T| \sim \lambda |T| for each T \in \mathbb{T}, then the full Kakeya maximal conjecture implies the multiplicity of a generic \delta-cube in X is O(m \lambda^{1-n}). So given a set X, after thinning X appropriately we can set M \lesssim m \lambda^{1-n} in the result above.
Let us try and use this technique to obtain an extension bound. Assume that \| f \|_{L^p(M)} = 1. Consider a wave packet decomposition f = \sum f_{\theta,\nu} at a scale R^{-1/2}, and thus E_S f = \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}} g_T, where T = \{ T_{\theta,\nu}: f_{\theta,\nu} \neq 0 \}. By pigeonholing, we may assume that there is B > 0 so that \| f_{\theta,\nu} \|_{L^\infty(M)} \sim B for all T \in \mathbb{T}. Let X be a set, which we may assume to be R^{1/2} discretized, we try and find X_0 \subset X with |X_0| \geq |X|/10 so that \| E_S f \|_{L^p(X_0)} \lesssim 1. We may assume X is R^{1/2} discretized, and by dyadic pigeonholing, we may find a dyadic scale \lambda so that X(T) = X \cap T satisfies |X(T)| \sim \lambda |T| for some \lambda \in [0,1] and all T \in \mathbb{T}. Because the functions \{ g_T \} are orthogonal on R^{1/2} cubes, they are also orthogonal on X, so
\| E_S f \|_{L^2(X)} \lesssim \left( \sum \| g_T \|_{L^2(X)}^2 \right)^{1/2} \sim (\lambda R)^{1/2} \left( \sum \| f_{\theta,\nu} \|_{L^2(M)}^2 \right)^{1/2} = (\lambda R)^{1/2} \| f \|_{L^2(M)}.
On the other hand, if p_{ST} is the Stein-Tomas exponent, we can find a set X_0 \subset X with |X_0| \geq |X|/10 so that M \lesssim m \lambda^{-(n-1)}. If we could somehow improve this bound so we could assume M \lesssim m \lambda^{- \frac{n-1}{2}}, then
\begin{split}
    \| E_S f \|_{L^{p_{ST}}(X_0)} &\lessapprox (M/m)^{ \frac{1}{n+1}} R^{-\frac{n-1}{2(n+1)}} \| f \|_{L^{p_{ST}}(X_0)}\\
    &\lessapprox (\lambda R)^{- 1/p_{ST}} \| f \|_{L^{p_{ST}}(X_0)}
\end{split}
Interpolating, we obtain that at the critical exponent for the restriction conjecture, i.e. for p = 2n/(n-1),
\| E_S f \|_{L^p(X_0)} \lesssim \| f \|_{L^p(X_0)}.
This is sufficient to completely prove the restriction conjecture.
In general, we will be able to obtain square root cancellation in \lambda by forcing X to satisfy a two-ends condition, and we can then use this to obtain restriction estimates. Given a set X satisfying the two-ends condition, with |X \cap T| \sim \lambda |T| for each T \in \mathbb{T}, the bush argument of Bourgain gives that |X| \gtrapprox R^{\frac{3n+1}{4}} \lambda, and so the average multiplicity of a cube Q in X is M \lesssim m \lambda^{- \frac{n-1}{n+1}} R^{-\frac{(n-1)^2}{4(n+1)}} \lambda^{-\frac{n-1}{n+1}}, which gives
\| E_S f \|_{L^{p_{ST}}(X_0)} \lessapprox R^{-\frac{(n-1)[3n+1]}{4(n+1)^2}} \lambda^{- \frac{n-1}{(n+1)^2}} \| f \|_{L^{p_{ST}}}
Interpolation between the L^2 estimate gives a result of the form
\| E_S f \|_{L^p(X_0)} \lessapprox \| f \|_{L^p(X_0)}
for p = \frac{2 + 2n(5n + 2)}{(n-1)(5n + 3)}. In particular, for n = 3 this gives p = 26/9 \approx 2.89.

1---General-Zettels/Wave-Packet-Decompos.title: Wave Packet Decomposition For Extension Operators
1---General-Zettels/Wave-Packet-Decompos.content:
Given any function f supported on a compact neighborhood of a curved hypersurface \Sigma, and each R > 0, we can break \Sigma down finitely overlapping caps \Theta, each having dimensions R^{-1/2} tangent to \Sigma, and dimension R^{-1} in the normal direction to \Sigma. If, for each \theta \in \Theta, we consider a family of tubes \mathbb{T}(\theta) pointing in the normal direction to the cap, with dimensions R^{1/2} by R, and then write \mathbb{T} = \bigcup \mathbb{T}(\theta), then we have an orthogonal decomposition f = \sum f_T, where for T \in \mathbb{T}(\theta) the following is true:

f_T is supported on \theta.
For x \in B_R, E_\Sigma f_T(x) \approx a_T \chi_T e^{2 \pi i \omega_\theta x}, where \omega_\theta is the center of \theta, |a_T| \sim R^{-1/2} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\Sigma)}, and \chi_T is smooth and adapted to T. In particular, \| E_\Sigma f_T \|_{L^2(B_R)} \sim R^{1/2} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\Sigma)}.
The functions E f_T are locally orthogonal on balls of radius R^{1/2}. That is, for any ball B of radius at least R^{1/2}, \| Ef \|_{L^2(B)} \approx \sum \| Ef_T \|_{L^2(B)}^2.

The method was widely brought to the attention by Bourgain’s use of the result in a 1991 paper (some claim he introduced the method, though it is also in use by Fefferman and Cordoba in their work on the restriction problem), and is highly similar to the second dyadic decomposition introduced in 1991 by Seeger, Sogge and Stein to analyze Fourier Integral Operators, i.e. it is precisely the scale at which the phase function in the extension operator linearizes, or equivalently, the largest rectangular boxes contained in a 1/R-neighborhood of the surface \Sigma.

2---Paper-Review/2026.01.04---Gan-He-Li-.filePath: 2 - Paper Review/2026.01.04 - Gan He Li Wu - On Local Smoothing Estimates for Wave Equations.md
2---Paper-Review/2026.01.04---Gan-He-Li-.title: 2026.01.04 - Gan He Li Wu - On Local Smoothing Estimates for Wave Equations
2---Paper-Review/2026.01.04---Gan-He-Li-.content:
Introduction
This paper obtains sharp estimates for local smoothing estimates for the wave equation, and related Fourier integral operators satisfying a positive definite cinematic curvature condition. In particular, the result proves a local smoothing estimate for the half-wave equation with respect to an arbitrary Riemannian metric. In this talk, however, we will mainly discuss how the proof works in the Euclidean case.
The local smoothing conjecture claims that for 2n/(n-1) < p < \infty, i.e. for 1/2 - 1/p > 1/2n, the operator
\tilde{\mathcal{F}} f(x,t) = (e^{i t \sqrt{-\Delta}} f)(x) = \int e^{i[\xi \cdot x + t |\xi|]} \widehat{f}(\xi)\; d\xi
satisfies \| \tilde{\mathcal{F}} f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n \times [-1,1])} \lesssim \| f \|_{L^p_\sigma(\mathbb{R}^n)} for \sigma > (n-1)|1/p - 1/2| - 1/p. If we define
\mathcal{F}^\lambda = e^{it \sqrt{-\Delta}} \circ \chi(\sqrt{-\Delta} / \lambda)
then it suffices to prove that
\| \mathcal{F}^\lambda f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}
^n \times [-1,1])} \lesssim \lambda^\sigma \| f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}.
By finite speed of propagation and translation invariance of the wave equation, it suffices to prove that for the unit ball B_1^{n+1} centered at the origin,
\| \mathcal{F}^\lambda f \|_{L^p(B_1^{n+1})} \lesssim \lambda^\sigma \| f \|_{L^p(B_2^n)}
If we define f_\lambda(x) = f(\lambda x), then
\begin{align*}
\mathcal{F}^\lambda f_\lambda(x,t) &= \left[ \left(e^{it \sqrt{-\Delta}} \circ \chi(\sqrt{-\Delta} / \lambda \right) f_\lambda \right] (x)\\
&= \int_{\mathbb{R}^n} e^{i [ \xi \cdot x + t |\xi| ]} \chi(|\xi| / \lambda) \widehat{f}_\lambda(\xi)\; d\xi\\
&= \int e^{\lambda i[\xi \cdot x + t |\xi|]} \chi(|\xi|) \widehat{f}(\xi)\; d\xi\\
&= \mathcal{F} f (\lambda x, \lambda t)
\end{align*}
where \mathcal{F} = \mathcal{F}^1. We thus see that the local smoothing conjecture is equivalent to the estimate
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})} \lessapprox R^{(n-1)(1/p - 1/2)} \| f \|_{L^p(B^n_{2R})}.
This paper proves the following result:
Theorem: Fix n \geq 1. Suppose
p > \begin{cases} 2 + \frac{8}{3n-3} &: n\ \text{odd}, \\ 2 + \frac{8}{3n - 2 - \omega(n)} &: n\ \text{even}. \end{cases}
where \omega(n) = 4(7n-2)/(3n^2+5n+14) = (28/3) n^{-1} + O(n^{-2}). Then the local-smoothing conjecture holds for this exponent.
We note these estimates are the sharpest possible in the class of half-wave propagators for the wave equation on a Riemannian manifold, as there are examples of Riemannian metrics which cause the local smoothing estimate to fail for p < 2 + 8/(3n-3) when n is odd, and for p < 2 + 8/(3n-2) when n is even.
In the notes that follow, we only focus on the proof in the odd case, in what follows. The proof in the even case is similar, but involving slightly different parameters.
Notation
We work with a series of scales
A \ll R^\delta \ll K_\circ \ll K \ll R^\varepsilon.
where the quantities occur at vastly different scales. More precisely:

A = (\log K)^2
\delta = \varepsilon^{1000}
K_\circ = R^{\varepsilon^{100}}
K = R^{\varepsilon^{50}}

Because of this, it is often useful to think of K as a relatively small parameter with respect to R, despite the fact that, when R is very large, K will also be very large.
We also will try and consistently use the following notation:





























SymbolNotationBA unit ball in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}.QA radius R^{1/2} ball in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}.TA 1 \times R^{1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{1/2} \times R plank in \mathbb{R}^{n+1}.\tauA 1 \times K^{-1} \times \cdots \times K^{-1} cap in \mathbb{R}^n\thetaA 1 \times R^{-1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{-1/2} cap in \mathbb{R}^n
Wave Packets and Pigeonholing Parameters
Like in many problems about oscillatory integrals, it is profitable in the study of local smoothing to perform a wave packet decomposition of the operator, into regions where the oscillatory integral is linearizable, and thus it’s behavior can be well understood.
For a given input f living in B_{2R}^n, if we wish to study \mathcal{F} f(x,t) on B_R^{n+1}, we may without loss of generality consider a wave packet decomposition f = \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}} f_T, where \mathbb{T} is a family of of 1 \times R^{1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{1/2} \times R planks which are dual to 1 \times R^{-1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{-1/2} \times R^{-1} caps aligned with the light cone \Gamma = \{ (\xi,\omega) \in \mathbb{R}^{n + 1} : |\xi| = |\omega| \}. The functions \{ f_T : T \in \mathbb{T} \} have the property that they are Fourier supported on the projection of the cap \theta(T) onto \mathbb{R}^n, and \mathcal{F} f_T is locally constant on the plank T, rapidly decaying outside of T.
Let X be a union of 1-balls. Since \mathcal{F} f is locally constant in space-time at a scale 1, we may choose such a set X so that

\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(X)} \sim \| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^d)}

By pigeonholing on both the set \mathbb{T} of tubes, and the set of balls X (as well as introducing a shading, though we will not go into this technicality), we may further assume that

For each T \in \mathbb{T}, \mathcal{F} f_T has amplitude \sim 1 on T, i.e. \| \mathcal{F} f_T \|_{L^r(\mathbb{R}^{n+1})} \sim |T|^{1/r} for 1 \leq r \leq \infty.
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B)} \sim \| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B')} for any unit balls B,B' \subset X.

We may also introduce the following statistics via pigeonholing:









































NotationDescription\muEach unit ball B \subset X broadly intersects \sim \mu planks in \mathbb{T}. We let \mathbb{T}(B) denote the set of all such planks. What we mean precisely is that if we consider a family of A caps of dimension 1 \times K^{-1} \times \cdots \times K^{-1}, and we remove all planks from \mathbb{T}(B) whose directions are contained in the union of these caps, we will still have \gtrsim \mu caps. Moreover, there exists a particular family such that, after removing planks from these directions, we will have \sim \mu caps.mEach R^{1/2} ball Q with X \cap Q \neq \emptyset intersects \sim m planks in \mathbb{T}. We let \mathbb{T}(Q) denote the set of all such planks.lFor each plank T \in \mathbb{T}, X \cap T intersects \sim l radius R^{1/2} balls. We let \mathbb{Q}(T) denote the set of all such balls.\lambdaFor each plank T \in \mathbb{T}, and each length R / K_\circ time-slice \omega, if X(T) \cap \omega \neq \emptyset then \|X(T) \cap \omega\| \sim \lambda.\sigmaEach R^{1/2} ball Q with X \cap Q \neq \emptyset has \|X \cap Q\| \sim \sigma.\betaA measure of the two-ends condition, which roughly implies that X(T) does not concentrate on small subsets of the plank T. For each T \in \mathbb{T}, we assume there are \sim \beta length R/K_\circ time-slices \omega such that X(T) \cap \omega \neq \emptyset.\rhoFor each R^{1/2} ball Q with X \cap Q \neq \emptyset, the set X \cap Q is covered by \sim \rho^{-1} \sigma slabs S of size 1 \times R^{1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{1/2}, and \|X \cap S\| \leq \rho for each such slab. We let \mathbb{S}(Q) denote the set of such slabs. We only use this statistic when n = 3.\nuFor each B \subset X, \left\| \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}(B)} \mathcal{F}_\lambda f_T \right\|_{L^p(B)} \sim \nu.
We will also assume broadness, i.e. for each unit ball B \subset X, there exists disjoint subsets \mathbb{T}_1(B), \dots, \mathbb{T}_k(B) of \mathbb{T}(B) so that \# \mathbb{T}_j(B) \gtrsim \mathbb{T}(B) and so that any sequence of k tubes chosen from these sets are quantitatively transverse.
Wang-Wu Refined-Decoupling Interpolation
The main method of proof in this theorem is a strategy initially introduced in Hong Wang and Shukun Wu’s paper Restriction estimates using decoupling theorems and two-ends Furstenberg inequalities. One nice feature of the method is that it neatly separates where the oscillatory part of the problem is utilized, and where the incidence part of the problem is utilized. The oscillatory properties of the wave are exploited using an interpolation between a family of different estimates depending on various statistics in the problem.
When n = 3, we exploit a refined decoupling estimate in an L^{p_D} norm, where p_D = 2(n+1)/(n-1) is the decoupling estimate for the cone, and an L^2 estimate exploiting orthogonality. The L^{p_D} estimate is efficient if the quantity \mu is small, i.e. if the planks in \mathbb{T} do not intersect X with high multiplicity. The L^2 norm is a useful estimate if X \cap T is concentrated in a small region of space. Incidence geometry can thus tell us that in any configuration of tubes, one of these conditions must be efficient if the other is inefficient, which is sufficient to get an L^p norm bound uniformly in the relevant parameters when the numerology is worked through.
When n \geq 5, rather than interpolating between L^2 and L^{p_D}, we instead interpolate between three bounds, a restricted decoupling L^{p_D} bound, an L^0 bound, and a L^{p_{\text{B}}} bound obtained using broad narrow analysis. But the idea is much the same.
Establishing A Two Ends Condition
In order to obtain incidence geometric estimates, it is often useful to assume a certain non-concentration condition on the plank \mathbb{T} with respect to the set X. That is, we want to assume X(T) is spread out along the plank T. Such a condition is often called a two-ends condition in the literature. Fortunately, we have a parameter \beta in our problem that measures precisely this, and so we want to reduce our argument to the case \beta \gg 1.
Such a condition is often obtained by performing an induction on scales. If, say \beta \leq 100, then we claim that we are not really looking at the problem at the ‘right scale’, and by zooming in, we can obtain our result for free by analyzing the problem at smaller scales.
Break B_R^{n+1} into balls B of radius R/K_\circ. Exploiting the inductive hypothesis on these balls, we may obtain a good bound for \| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B)} for each ball B. If we can combine these bounds efficiently, then we may obtain an efficient bound on \| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})}, completing the proof.
However, problems can occur if we do not take the right inductive hypothesis in the argument. Suppose we take the naive approach, letting C(R) be the best constant such that for all functions f, the bound
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})}^p \leq C(R) \| f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}^p.
holds. If \beta \leq 1, then for each ball B of radius R / K_\circ, if we define f_B = \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}(B)} f_T, then by induction,
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B)}^p \leq C(R/K_\circ) \| f_B \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}^p.
Summing in B, we conclude that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})}^p \leq C(R/K_\circ) \sum \| f_B \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}^p.
If we could efficiently bound \sum \| f_B \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}^p by \| f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}^p, then this would complete the proof. We can get such a bound when p = 2, precisely because each plank T in the wave packet decomposition of f contributes to O(1) of the balls B. But when p > 2 putting the functions \{ f_B \} together does not work so well.
One alternative is to alter the inductive hypothesis by proving a harder result. One option would be the following: let C(R) be the best possible constant so that for all functions f,
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})}^p \leq C(R) \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 \| f \|_{L^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)}^{p - 2}.
This is a natural choice, because if f is the characteristic function of some set X, then
\| f \|_{L^p(\mathbb{R}^n)}^p = \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 \| f \|_{L^\infty(\mathbb{R}^n)}^{p - 2} = |X|.
Thus this bound is sufficient to obtain a restricted L^p bound on the operator \mathcal{F}, which by interpolation is efficient to obtain the bounds of interest since we are working away from an endpoint. This option seems better since we have an L^2 norm, which allows us to recombine the pieces \{ f_B \} in a nice way. But the L^\infty norm does not combine in such a nice way, since the L^\infty norm is not stable under taking subsets of tubes.
In analogous problems, like restriction, a successful fix is to work with an even stronger inductive hypothesis: we let C(R) denote the best constant so that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})}^p \leq C(R) \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 \sup\nolimits_B \| f \|_{Ł^2(B)}^{p - 2},
where B ranges over a natural cover of B_R^{n+1} by balls at a scale natural to the problem of study, and where Ł^2(B) denotes an averaged L^2 norm over the ball B. We can bound \sup_{B'} \| f_B \|_{Ł^2(B')} in terms of \sup\nolimits_B \| f \|_{Ł^2(B)}, where B' ranges over a lower scale of balls, which is sufficient to obtain the required results. But this approach has problems in other parts of the argument, because the quantity \sup\nolimits_B \| f \|_{Ł^2(B)}^{p - 2} is not stable under Lorenz rescaling, the natural symmetries of the wave equation required to perform an induction on scales. In particular, the broad-narrow analysis no longer goes through.
One key introduction in this paper is replacement of \sup\nolimits_B \| f \|_{Ł^2(B)} with a new quantity the paper calls a wave packet density, which is stable under Lorentz rescaling. This is a Lorentz invariant variant of an L^2 average over R^{1/2} planks, as well as all their Lorentz dilations. Heuristically, we have
W(f,B_R^{n+1}) = \sup\nolimits_{s \in [R^{-1/2},1]} \sup\nolimits_{U \subset B_R} \left( |U|^{-1} \int \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}[U]} |e^{it \sqrt{-\Delta}} f_T|^2 \right)^{1/2}
Here U is an Rs^2 \times Rs \times \cdots \times Rs \times R plank lying along the light cone, for some s \in [R^{-1/2},1], and \mathbb{T}[U] is the set of 1 \times R^{1/2} \times R planks contained in U. If all the wave packets in the decomposition of f are assumed to have amplitude 1, then for each U the inner quantity is the square root of |U|^{-1} |T| times the total number of wave packets in U.
Some elementary inequalities for the wave density:

W(f,B_R^{n+1}) \lesssim \| f \|_{Ł^2(B_R)} \leq \| f \|_{L^\infty(B_R)}.
W(f,B_R^{n+1}) \gtrsim \max(1, R^{- \frac{n+1}{2}} \# \mathbb{T}) \gtrsim R^{- \frac{n+1}{4}} \# \mathbb{T}^{1/2}. The latter inequality follows if U = B_R^{n+1}, in which \mathbb{T}[U] consists of all tubes.
For 1 \leq r \leq R^{1 - 10 \delta}, and all B_r^{n+1} \subset B_R^{n+1}, \mathcal{W}(f,B_r^{n+1}) \lesssim R^{O(\delta)} (R/r)^{\frac{n-1}{2}} \mathcal{W}(f,B_R^{n+1}). This simply follows since the smaller scale wave packets are accounted for in the L^2 norm of the larger wave packet by orthogonality, and the loss only occurs from the difference in normalization factors.

If we let C(R) denote the best constant so that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})}^p \leq C(R) \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 W(f,B_R^{n+1})^{p-2},
Suppose we have a function f which attains this bound, i.e. so that the left hand and right hand side are comparable, and moreover, assume that for this input we have \beta \leq 100. Then
\begin{split}
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B)}^p &\leq C(R/K_\circ) \sum\nolimits_B \| f_B \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 W(f_B, B)^{p-2}\\
&\leq R^\delta C(R/K_\circ) K_\circ^{\frac{n-1}{2}(p-2)} \left( \sum\nolimits_B \| f_B \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 \right) W(f,B_R^{n+1})^{p-2}\\
&\sim R^\delta C(R/K_\circ) K_\circ^{\frac{n-1}{2}(p-2)} \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 W(f,B_R^{n+1})^{p-2}.
\end{split}
Thus
C(R) \lesssim R^\delta C(R/K_\circ) K_\circ^{\frac{n-1}{2} (p-2)}.
This is a sufficient recurrence to prove by induction that for each \varepsilon > 0, C(R) \leq C_\varepsilon R^{(n-1)(p/2 - 1) + \varepsilon}. Thus our proof is complete if \beta \leq 100, and we may assume that \beta \geq 100 in what follows, which will prove more helpful in the incidence geometry that follows.
L^{p_D} Estimates Using Refined Decoupling
Let us start by discussing the refined decoupling theorem.
Theorem (Refined Decoupling): If p_D = 2(n+1)/(n-1), then
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^{p_D}(X)}^{p_D} \lessapprox \mu^{\frac{2}{n-1}} \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \| \mathcal{F} f_T \|_{L^{p_D}(B_R^{n+1})}^{p_D}.
We’ll take the theorem as a black box in what follows. The theorem is more efficient if \mu is relatively small, i.e. if tubes intersect with low multiplicity.
We want to take this estimate, and convert the right hand side into something that is more easy to interpolate with other estimates. Since \mathcal{F} f_T has amplitude \sim 1 on T,
\| \mathcal{F} f_T \|_{L^{p_D}(B_R^{n+1})}^{p_D} \sim |T| = R^{\frac{n+1}{2}} \quad\text{and}\quad \| f_T \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)} \sim R^{\frac{n-1}{2}},
so \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)} \sim R^{\frac{n-1}{2}} (\# \mathbb{T})^{1/2} holds by orthogonality. This means that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^{p_D}(X)}^{p_D} \lessapprox \mu^{\frac{2}{n-1}} (\# \mathbb{T}) R^{\frac{n+1}{2}} = \big( R \mu^{\frac{2}{n-1}} \big) \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^2
L^2 Bounds When n = 3 Using Slab Orthogonality
Let Q be a radius R^{1/2} ball. Using \rho-regularity, X \cap Q is covered by a family \mathbb{S}(Q) of K^2 \times R^{1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{1/2} slabs S with \# \mathbb{S}(Q) \sim \rho^{-1} \sigma, and |S \cap X| \lesssim \rho for any such slab. We may assume that this cover is finitely overlapping. Thus
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(S)}^2 \lesssim \sum\nolimits_S \| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(S)}^2
If we let V be the vector space parallel to the slab S, then using a 4-broad-narrow analysis,
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(S)}^2 \lessapprox \sum\nolimits_{B} \max\nolimits_{\tau \not \in V} \int_B |\mathcal{F} f_\tau |^2,
where B ranges over balls of radius K^{-2} in a cover of S. Since there are only K^{O(1)} caps \tau, we may pigeonhole, finding a single cap \tau such that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(S)}^2 \lessapprox \sum\nolimits_B \int_B |\mathcal{F} f_\tau|^2 = \| \mathcal{F} f_\tau \|_{L^2(S)}^2.
Since all the caps in \tau are quantitatively transverse to S, as guaranteed by the fact that \tau \not \in V, we have an L^2 orthogonality bound (see the Lemma later on in the section) of the form
\| \mathcal{F} f_\tau \|_{L^2(S)}^2 \lessapprox \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}(Q)} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2.
Now we sum over all the slabs S. Since \# \mathbb{S}(Q) \sim \rho^{-1} \sigma, and since each plank intersects \sim l radius R^{1/2} balls, we sum up and conclude that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(B_R^{n+1})}^2 \lessapprox (l \sigma / \rho) \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}(Q)} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 = (l \sigma / \rho) \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2.
Lemma: Let S be a 1 \times R^{1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{1/2} slab parallel to an n-dimensional vector space V. Let \tau \subset \mathbb{R}^n be a 1 \times K^{-1} \times \cdots \times K^{-1} cap, whose normal vector on the cone makes an angle greater than K^{-2} with V. Then for any family \mathbb{T} of 1 \times R^{1/2} \times \cdots \times R^{1/2} \times R planks whose direction is contained in \tau, 
\left\| \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \mathcal{F} f_T \right\|_{L^2(S)}^2 \lessapprox \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2.
Proof: Write \mathbb{T} = \bigcup_\theta \mathbb{T}_\theta, where \mathbb{T}_\theta are the family of tubes pointing in the direction \theta. Write f_\theta = \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}_\theta} f_T. Purely by the spatial supports of the functions \mathcal{F} f_T, for any set $
\| f_\theta \|_{L^2(S)}^2 \lesssim \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}_\theta} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2,
and so it suffices to study the interactions between the different functions \{ f_\theta \}. Incurring a K^{O(1)} factor, we may assume that the directions \{ \theta \} lie within a 1 \times K^{-10} cap.
The idea is simple. If \chi_S is a smooth bump function adapted to S, then
\begin{split}
\left\| \sum\nolimits_\theta f_\theta \right\|_{L^2(S)}^2 &\leq \left\| \sum\nolimits_\theta \chi_S f_\theta \right\|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2\\
&\leq \sum\nolimits_\theta \| \chi_S f_\theta \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 + \sum\nolimits_{\theta_1,\theta_2} \langle \chi_S f_{\theta_1}, \chi_S f_{\theta_2} \rangle.
\end{split}
We cannot use the disjoint supports of the Fourier transforms of f_{\theta_1} and f_{\theta_2} directly because of the introductions of the cutoffs \chi_S, which blend the Fourier supports of the tubes \{ f_\theta \}, thickening the support in the normal direction to the slab S by O(1), and thickening the support in the parallel directions to the slab by O(R^{-1/2}). However, we claim that if \theta_1 and \theta_2 are sufficiently close together, as we have guaranteed by the localization in the last paragraph, then the functions f_{\theta_1} and f_{\theta_2} can only significantly interact if |\theta_1 - \theta_2| \lessapprox R^{-1/2}, which is sufficient to prove
To see this, we note that f_{\theta_1} has Fourier support near (\xi_1, |\xi_1|), and f_{\theta_2} has Fourier support near (\xi_2, |\xi_2|), then because \xi_1 and \xi_2 are assumed close together, the difference (\xi_1 - \xi_2, |\xi_1| - |\xi_2|) b between the two vectors lies along the tangent space to the light cone at \xi_2. By assumption, the angle between the normal vector to this tangent space and the vector space V is greater than K^{-2}. This implies the projection of (\xi_1 - \xi_2, |\xi_1| - |\xi_2|) onto V has length \gtrsim K^{-2} |\xi_1 - \xi_2|. But the localization \chi_S only thickens the Fourier support in directions along V by R^{-1/2}. So we conclude the two functions only interact if |\xi_1 - \xi_2| \lesssim K^2 R^{-1/2}, which proves the claim. □
L^2 Bounds When n \geq 5 Using L^{p_B} and L^0 Bounds
When n \geq 5, we also obtain an L^{p_B} bound using broad narrow analysis. This bound is obtained using a polynomial partitioning argument of Ou and Wang to study restriction to the cone.
Theorem (Broad-Narrow Analysis): If p_B = \frac{2(n + k_n + 1)}{n + k_n - 1}, where k_n = \frac{n + 5}{2}, then
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^{p_B}(B_R^{n+1})} \lessapprox \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}.
Since p_B \geq 2, we can obtain an L^2 bound from this result if we have good bounds on the support of our inputs, i.e. we have a good L^0 bound. For each radius R^{1/2} ball Q, we use that |X \cap Q| \sim \sigma to conclude that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(X \cap Q)} \lessapprox \sigma^{1/2 - 1/p_B} \left( \sum\nolimits_{T \in \mathbb{T}(Q)} \| f_T \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^2 \right)^{1/2}
and then we sum over Q, using that each plank intersects \sim l radius R^{1/2} balls to conclude that
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(X)} \lessapprox l^{1/2} \sigma^{1/2 - 1/p_B} \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}.
Incidence Estimates
We have two bounds, an L^{p_D} estimate which is efficient if \mu is small, and an L^2 estimate which is efficient when l and \sigma is small (and \rho if n = 3). Interpolating, we will obtain a bound which is uniform in all parameters if, at a high level, we can justify that in all situations, either \mu is small, or l and \sigma is small. That is, either tubes intersect X with high multiplicity, or the union of the tubes takes up a large amount of X. Thus we are left with an incidence geometry problem.
We begin with a hairbrush argument.
Lemma: l m \mu \lesssim \# \mathbb{T}
Proof: We obtain some initial bounds by employing a hairbrush argument. Each ball in X is contained in \sim \mu planks, and so the total number of tube-plank incidences is \sim |X| \mu. Conversely, each plank intersects \sim \beta \lambda balls, so the total number of tube-plank incidences is \sim \beta \lambda \# \mathbb{T}. We thus conclude that |X| \mu \sim \beta \lambda \# \mathbb{T} by double counting.
On the other hand, if we pick a plank T_0 \in \mathbb{T} and define a hairbrush \mathbb{H}(T_0) as all planks T \in \mathbb{T} transverse to T_0 such that X(T) \cap X(T_0) \neq \emptyset. Such planks are essentially disjoint from one another (provided \beta is sufficiently large, say, \beta \geq 100). So
|X| \gtrsim \sum_{T \in \mathbb{H}(T_0)} |X(T)| \gtrsim l m \beta \lambda,
where we used that |X(T)| \sim \beta \lambda and \# \mathbb{H}(T_0) \sim lm.
We thus conclude that l m \beta \lambda \lesssim \beta \lambda \mu^{-1} \# \mathbb{T}, i.e. l m \mu \lesssim \# \mathbb{T}. □
If we can efficiently lower bound m by the parameters \mu, l, \sigma, and \rho, then we obtain the required incidence geometry estimates.
When n = 3, we can utilize the 4-broadness of the incidences under study to obtain the bound
m \gtrsim \mu \sigma / \rho
which is sufficient to complete the incidence geometry in this case. This estimate follows because \rho m upper bounds the number of broad incidence in the problem due to (6.11), and \mu \sigma lower bounds the number of broad incidences due to (6.9). But I’m not quite sure how (6.11) is justified right now.
When n \geq 5, we cannot use such a high amount of broadness since our broad-narrow argument is done with k-broadness, where k = (n+5)/2. When n \geq 5 we instead use a trilinear argument to obtain a bound m \gtrsim \mu (\sigma R^{- \frac{n+1 - k_n}{2}})^{1/k_n}, which is sufficient.
Instead, we use a trilinear argument. We use broadness to find, for each unit ball B contained in X, three distinct K^{-1}-caps \tau_1, \tau_2,\tau_3 which are transverse to one another, and with \# \mathbb{T}_{\tau_i}(B) \gtrsim \mu. Then for any caps T_i \in \mathbb{T}_{\tau_i}(B), |T_1 \cap T_2 \cap T_3| \lessapprox R^{\frac{n-2}{2}}.
\begin{split}
\mu^3 \sigma &= \mu^3 |X \cap Q|\\
&\lessapprox \sum\nolimits_B \int_B \prod\nolimits\nolimits_{j \leq 3} \left( \sum_{T \in \mathbb{T}_j} 1_{T_j} \right)\\
&\sim \sum\nolimits_{T_j \in \mathbb{T}_{\tau_j}(B)} |T_1 \cap T_2 \cap T_3|\\
&\lessapprox R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} \# \mathbb{T}(Q)^3 = R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} m^3
\end{split}
So \mu^3 \sigma \lesssim R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} m^3.
We plug this lower bound for m into the hairbrush estimate l m \mu \lesssim \# \mathbb{T} to conclude that \mu \lesssim l^{-1/2} (\# \mathbb{T})^{1/2} ( R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} \sigma^{-1} )^{1/6}, which is sufficient.
We obtain that \mu \lessapprox (\# \mathbb{T})^{1/2} l^{-1/2} \min(1, (R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} \sigma^{-1})^{1/6}) using Lemma 7.5 (follows bound if beta is large) which follows from \# \mathbb{B} \gtrapprox l m \lambda (item (v) of list 1) and \# \mathbb{B} \mu \lesssim \# \mathbb{T} \beta \lambda (item (2) of list 3) and \sigma \lessapprox R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} m^3 \mu^{-3} (item 4 of List 3) and m \geq \mu (item (d) list List 1)
TODO: HOW TO GET \mu \lessapprox (\# \mathbb{T})^{1/2} l^{-1/2} \min( 1, (\rho / \sigma)^{1/2}, (R^{\frac{n-2}{2}} / \sigma)^{1/6} ).
Interpolation To Finish The Proof
Consider the case n = 3. We take the bound
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^{p_D}(B_R^{n+1})} \lessapprox \left( R \mu^{\frac{2}{n-1}} \right)^{2/p_D} \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^d)}^{2/p_D}
and the bound
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^2(B_R^{n+1})} \lessapprox (l \sigma / \rho)^{1/2} \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}.
and interpolate. Interpolating between these parameters, and then introducing W(f,B_R^{n+1}) using the trivial bound W(f,B_R^{n+1}) \gtrsim R^{- \frac{n+1}{4}} (\# \mathbb{T})^{1/2}, we get
\| \mathcal{F} f \|_{L^p(B_R^{n+1})} \lessapprox R^{(n-1)(1/2 - 1/p)} \mu^{1/5} (l \sigma / \rho)^{1/10} (\# \mathbb{T})^{-1/10} \Big[ \| f \|_{L^2(\mathbb{R}^n)}^{3/5} W(f,B_R^{n+1})^{2/5} \Big].
The power of R is appropriate here, so our proof will be complete if we can justify that l \sigma \mu^2 \lesssim \rho (\# \mathbb{T}). For n = 5, a similar bound emerges, but with a much more complicated numerology because we are interpolating between three different estimates.
